sentences.base_language: My alarm goes off at six thirty.
sentences.target_language: Mon réveil sonne à six heures et demie.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Mon réveil** — *réveil* (alarm clock) is masculine, so the possessive adjective is **mon**.
**Sonne** comes from *sonner* (to ring / to go off) — a regular -er verb used for bells and alarms.
**Six heures et demie** (6:30) — time expressions: *et quart* (quarter past), *et demie* (half past), *moins le quart* (quarter to). Note: *demi* agrees with *heure* (feminine) in *six heures et **demie***, but when used alone as a noun: *il est midi et **demi***.

sentences.base_language: I get up and go to the bathroom.
sentences.target_language: Je me lève et je vais dans la salle de bain.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Se lever** (to get up) is a reflexive verb with a spelling change: the stem vowel gains a grave accent before silent endings: je me **lève**, tu te lèves, il se lève (compare: nous nous **levons** — no accent).
**Dans la salle de bain** — **dans** (in/into) is used for rooms: *dans la cuisine*, *dans la chambre*. The room name *salle de bain* is feminine.
**La salle de bain** vs. **les toilettes** — the bathroom (where you wash) vs. the toilet (where you go to the toilet) are typically separate in French homes.

**Prendre une douche** (to take a shower) — French uses **prendre** (to take) for shower and bath: *prendre une douche*, *prendre un bain*.
**Prendre** is an irregular verb: je **prends**, tu prends, il prend, nous **prenons**, vous **prenez**, ils **prennent**.
**Une douche** is feminine; **un bain** is masculine — always learn nouns with their article.

**Je me lave les cheveux** — with reflexive verbs + body parts, French uses the **definite article** (les) rather than a possessive adjective: *je me lave **les** cheveux* (not *mes* cheveux).
The reflexive pronoun **me** already implies possession — it is understood that you are washing your own hair.
**Les cheveux** (hair) is always plural in French — *un cheveu* (a single hair), *les cheveux* (hair in general).

sentences.base_language: I dry off and get dressed.
sentences.target_language: Je me sèche et je m'habille.
sentences.grammarNote: Notice the reflexive pattern again: se sécher (to dry off), s'habiller (to get dressed). These are all things you do to yourself, so French uses reflexive verbs.

**Je me brosse les dents** follows the same pattern as *je me lave les cheveux*: reflexive verb + definite article for body parts.
The reflexive pronoun **me** indicates the action is done to oneself; **les dents** (the teeth) uses **les** instead of *mes*.
**Une dent** (a tooth) is feminine; the plural is **les dents**. *La brosse à dents* = the toothbrush; *le dentifrice* = toothpaste.

sentences.base_language: I look at myself in the mirror.
sentences.target_language: Je me regarde dans le miroir.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Je me regarde** — *se regarder* (to look at oneself) is reflexive: the subject and object are the same person.
Contrast: *je regarde quelqu'un* (I look at someone else — not reflexive) vs. *je **me** regarde* (I look at myself — reflexive).
**Dans le miroir** (in the mirror) — **dans** is used for looking into a reflective surface or container: *dans le miroir*, *dans la glace*, *dans l'eau*.

**Prêt/prête** (ready) is an adjective that agrees with the subject: *je suis prêt* (male speaker), *je suis prête* (female speaker).
The notation **prêt(e)** is a convention used in learning materials to show both forms in one entry.
**Je suis + adjective** (I am + adjective) — *être* is used for describing states and qualities, both temporary and permanent.
